persona.communication_style: Prefers grounded, ethics-forward conversation that takes both craft and labor seriously, with concrete examples from concept-art and film pipelines. Responds best to specifics about training-data provenance, licensing, contracts, working conditions, and union-equivalent protections — and to tools that treat artists as collaborators with rights, not as a free corpus. Dislikes vague 'democratization' rhetoric, tools whose data origin is hidden, and any framing that separates technical capability from labor consequence.
persona.name: Karla Ortiz

occupation.description: Karla Ortiz is a concept artist, illustrator, and creative consultant whose film work includes Marvel Studios productions and other major studio projects, alongside an independent fine-art practice. She is a public advocate for artist rights in the age of generative AI — a named plaintiff in artist class-action litigation against generative-AI companies, a co-founder of the Concept Art Association's policy efforts, and a frequent speaker on consent, compensation, and provenance. She evaluates AI image tools through the dual lens of working concept artist and labor advocate: whether the dataset is consensual and licensed, whether the tool augments craft or replaces livelihoods, whether the platform's terms protect or exploit artists, and whether outputs respect attribution.
occupation.organization: Independent concept artist and illustrator working with film studios, publishers, and galleries; co-founder of the Concept Art Association's advocacy effort; named plaintiff in generative-AI artist litigation
occupation.title: Concept artist, illustrator, fine artist, and artist-rights advocate

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Deliver film-grade concept art and key visuals under studio NDAs and deadlines.
jobs_to_be_done: Maintain an independent fine-art practice alongside studio work.
jobs_to_be_done: Advocate for opt-in datasets, licensing, and compensation in generative-AI policy.
jobs_to_be_done: Support fellow concept artists navigating contracts that increasingly include AI clauses.
jobs_to_be_done: Evaluate new tools for whether they meet a basic ethical floor before any aesthetic conversation.
jobs_to_be_done: Speak publicly — to press, to legislators, to studios — about the labor reality of generative AI.
jobs_to_be_done: Preserve attribution, provenance, and licensing trails on her own work end-to-end.
pains: Generative tools trained on scraped artist catalogs without consent or compensation.
pains: Studios pressuring concept artists to use AI tools whose data origin is undisclosed.
pains: Contracts that quietly grant training rights on submitted concept art.
pains: 'Democratization' rhetoric that obscures the redistribution of value away from working artists.
pains: AI outputs that displace junior concept artists, eroding the on-ramp into the industry.
pains: Platforms whose 'opt-out' is performative rather than enforced.
pains: Public discourse that treats artist concerns as luddism rather than labor.
adoption_yes_if: Training data is fully opt-in, licensed, documented, and auditable.
adoption_yes_if: The tool refuses to invoke living artists by name as style prompts.
adoption_yes_if: It augments concept-art workflow (thumbnails, light studies, reference) without producing finals that compete with artists.
adoption_yes_if: Platform terms protect artists' rights to their own training-data status and outputs.
adoption_yes_if: Studios can use it under existing union-equivalent agreements without eroding labor protections.
adoption_yes_if: Outputs carry clear attribution and provenance metadata.
adoption_yes_if: The company behind the tool engages substantively with artist-rights organizations.
rejection_no_if: Training data is scraped, undisclosed, or relies on opt-out instead of opt-in.
rejection_no_if: The tool can imitate living artists by name.
rejection_no_if: Studios are using it to displace junior concept artists or to extract free training from submitted work.
rejection_no_if: Terms of service strip artists of rights or shift liability onto users.
rejection_no_if: 'Ethics' is a marketing line rather than an enforced practice.
rejection_no_if: The platform pretends labor consequences are someone else's problem.
rejection_no_if: It frames artist objections as obstacles rather than as legitimate inputs.
anti_goals: She does not want her work, or any artist's, used to train models without consent.
anti_goals: She does not want to legitimize tools whose foundations are unethical, no matter how capable.
anti_goals: She does not want generative AI to hollow out the concept-art labor market.
anti_goals: She does not want to debate the existence of artist rights in every conversation.
anti_goals: She does not want studios to use AI as a quiet wage-pressure mechanism.
anti_goals: She does not want a future where attribution and provenance are optional.
anti_goals: She does not want technical capability discussed in isolation from labor reality.

recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as 'Is the dataset opt-in?', 'Where is the consent?', 'Who benefits from this output?', 'What happens to the junior artists?', 'Is the opt-out real or performative?', 'What does this do to the labor market?', 'Whose work trained this?', 'Is this augmenting craft or replacing it?', 'What do the terms of service actually say?', and 'Ethics is not a marketing line.'
